worth_it_explanation: Seat controls decide which buyers and bidders reach a publisher's inventory through PubMatic and its OpenWrap header-bidding wrapper, so they sit directly between the publisher's supply and the demand that pays for it. A control list that grows by accretion, with blocks added during incidents and never reviewed, can quietly cut off legitimate demand and depress fill while nobody connects the lower revenue to the list. The check is a review of allowlist, blocklist, and bidder settings the publisher already maintains, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific buyer or bidder the publisher can re-examine.
intro: Seat controls govern which buyers and demand partners are allowed to bid on a publisher's inventory through PubMatic and its OpenWrap wrapper. The same controls that protect a publisher from a problem buyer can also, when they are too broad or left unreviewed, suppress demand that the publisher would want competing in the auction. These checks confirm that the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder settings are deliberate and current, and that the blocks in place are scoped tightly enough to keep legitimate demand and fill intact.

audit_checks.title: Buyer and bidder controls are not maintained as a deliberate, reviewed list
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder settings decide which demand competes for inventory. When they accrete over time without review, blocks added for a past reason stay in place after the reason is gone, and bidders sit enabled or disabled with no record of why, so the publisher cannot tell whether the current list still reflects an intentional demand strategy or a pile of stale decisions.

educate.base:
Seat controls in PubMatic and its OpenWrap header-bidding wrapper decide which buyers and demand partners are allowed to bid on a publisher's inventory. A buyer allowlist names the seats permitted to compete. A blocklist names the seats held out, whether for brand-safety reasons, a payment dispute, or a category the publisher does not want on its pages. Bidder management on the wrapper side decides which demand partners are switched on for the auction at all. Together these settings define the shape of demand reaching the inventory, so the value they protect depends on them staying current.

The risk is not that controls exist. The risk is a list that grows by accretion and is never reviewed. A buyer blocked during an incident two years ago stays blocked after the incident is resolved. A bidder switched off during a test stays off after the test ends. A blocklist copied from another property carries blocks that never applied to this inventory. Each entry made sense when it was added, but the list as a whole drifts away from any deliberate strategy, and the publisher loses the thread of why each block is there.

OpenWrap is open source and built on Prebid, so the bidder-management mechanics follow the Prebid model, where a host decides which adapters are enabled for the auction and can scope them by region. PubMatic's own console adds buyer-level allowlist and blocklist controls on top of the wrapper. The audit question is whether the publisher treats these as a reviewed list with a reason recorded for each entry, or as a set-and-forget configuration that no one has read in a long time.

A few signs point to controls that are not maintained intentionally:
  • Buyer blocks exist with no record of who added them, when, or why
  • Bidders are disabled on the wrapper with no note explaining whether the block is still needed
  • The blocklist was inherited from another property or a default template and never reconciled against this inventory
  • No periodic review of the allowlist, blocklist, and bidder settings is on the calendar
You may want to confirm that each buyer block and bidder setting traces back to a current, stated reason. One approach is to export the active controls, walk each entry against its justification, and retire the ones whose reason no longer holds.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, blocklists often grow during brand-safety incidents, for example a buyer running a creative that clashed with editorial content. You may want to date each block and revisit it on a schedule. A buyer blocked for one bad creative may now be demand the publisher wants back in the auction.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, some blocks exist to keep competing retailers or conflicting categories off product pages. You may want to confirm those category and competitor blocks still match the current merchandising relationships. A block set against a former competitor can suppress demand after the business relationship has changed.

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for buyer allowlists and blocklists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active buyer allowlist, blocklist, and bidder settings, and for each entry look for a recorded reason and date so you can tell deliberate controls from stale ones.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic console
steps: Open the buyer or seat controls in the PubMatic console and list every active allowlist and blocklist entry.
For each blocked buyer, record whether a reason and a date are documented anywhere, and who added it.
Note any entry with no recorded reason, which marks a block that cannot be tied back to a current decision.

openwrap_config.label: OpenWrap wrapper configuration
steps: Review the OpenWrap wrapper configuration and list which bidders are enabled and which are disabled for the auction.
For each disabled bidder, record whether a note explains why it is off and whether the reason still applies.
Cross-check the enabled bidders against the demand partners the publisher intends to run, and flag any mismatch between intent and configuration.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active buyer allowlist, blocklist, and bidder-management entry alongside its documented reason and date. A control set where every entry traces to a current, stated reason and a review cadence is on record passes. A set where blocks or disabled bidders exist with no recorded reason, or where the list was inherited and never reconciled, is a fail recorded with the specific entries that lack justification.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each buyer block and bidder setting with its reason and date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the buyer and bidder controls on {{site_name}} are not maintained as a deliberate, reviewed list. Several blocks and disabled bidders carry no recorded reason or date. Controls that accrete over time drift away from any current demand strategy, so a block added for a past incident or a bidder switched off during a test can keep cutting demand long after the reason is gone. {{voice_recommend}} exporting the active allowlist, blocklist, and bidder settings, walking each entry against its justification, retiring the ones whose reason no longer holds, and putting a periodic review on the calendar so the list stays tied to a stated intent.

audit_checks.title: Blocks are scoped so broadly that they suppress legitimate demand and fill
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block that is wider than the problem it was meant to solve removes demand the publisher would have wanted. Blocking an entire SSP to keep out one bad buyer, or applying a narrow incident block across all inventory, thins the auction so fewer bids compete, which lowers clearing price and fill on inventory that had nothing to do with the original reason.

educate.base:
A block is a blunt instrument when it is scoped wider than the problem it was meant to solve. Suppose a single buyer ran a creative that violated a publisher's brand-safety standard. The tight response blocks that buyer. The broad response blocks the entire demand partner or SSP the buyer came through, which removes every other buyer on that path along with the one at fault. The broad response feels safer in the moment, but it thins the demand competing for the inventory far beyond what the incident required.

Fewer bidders in the auction means fewer bids per impression, and fewer bids mean a lower clearing price and, on some impressions, no winning bid at all. So an over-broad block shows up later as softer fill and a lower average price on inventory that was never connected to the original reason. The publisher set out to protect the inventory and instead suppressed legitimate demand against it, and because the cause and the symptom are separated by weeks, the connection is easy to miss.

OpenWrap and Prebid let a host scope demand controls at the bidder level and, in some cases, by region, so a block can be aimed at the specific path that caused the problem rather than swept across everything. PubMatic's buyer controls similarly allow a block at the seat level rather than the partner level. The audit question is whether each block is scoped to the actual source of the problem, or whether broad blocks are doing damage well beyond their intent.

A few signs point to blocks that are scoped too broadly:
  • An entire SSP or demand partner is blocked when the documented reason names a single buyer or creative
  • An incident block was applied across all inventory rather than the placement or category where the problem appeared
  • Fill rate or bid density dropped on inventory after a block was added, with no other change to explain it
  • The number of bidders competing per impression is low relative to comparable inventory, which suggests demand is being filtered out upstream
You may want to confirm that each block is no wider than the problem it addresses. One approach is to compare bid density and fill on inventory affected by a block against comparable unblocked inventory, then narrow any block whose scope is removing more demand than its reason calls for.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a brand-safety block prompted by one advertiser's creative can end up excluding a whole class of demand if it is applied at the partner level. You may want to scope the block to the specific buyer and, where the concern is editorial fit, to the sections where it matters, so general-interest inventory keeps its full demand.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a category block meant to keep one competitor off product pages can suppress unrelated demand if it is applied across the whole property. You may want to limit such blocks to the product or category pages where the conflict is real, so editorial and lower-conflict inventory keeps competing for the full set of buyers.

learn_more.source_title: Module: Supply Chain Object for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for scoping buyer and seat blocks is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each active block against the reason behind it, and read fill and bid density on the affected inventory to see whether a block is removing more demand than its reason calls for.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic console
steps: List the active blocks and, for each, record whether it is scoped at the buyer or seat level or at the SSP or demand-partner level.
Compare the scope of each block against its documented reason, and flag any block that is wider than the buyer, creative, or category the reason names.
Note any partner-level or property-wide block whose reason points to a single buyer or a single placement, which marks a candidate for narrowing.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report on inventory affected by a block, with fill rate, average eCPM, and bids per impression where available.
Compare that inventory against comparable unblocked inventory to see whether the block coincides with lower fill or thinner bid density.
Record any case where a block lines up with a measurable drop in demand on inventory that the block's reason did not concern.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block with its scope, its documented reason, and the fill and bid density on the affected inventory compared against comparable unblocked inventory. Blocks scoped to the specific buyer, creative, or placement the reason names pass. A block scoped broader than its reason, especially one that coincides with lower fill or thinner demand on inventory the reason did not concern, is a fail recorded with the block, its scope, and the demand effect observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block with its scope, its reason, and the fill and bid density on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some blocks on {{site_name}} are scoped more broadly than the problem they address, for example a demand partner blocked when the reason names a single buyer, and the affected inventory shows softer fill and thinner bid density as a result. A block wider than its reason removes legitimate demand, which thins the auction and lowers clearing price and fill on inventory that had nothing to do with the original problem. {{voice_recommend}} narrowing each over-broad block to the specific buyer, creative, or placement its reason names, then rechecking fill and bid density on the affected inventory to confirm the demand returns once the block is scoped to its actual cause.
